questions.question: Match each design to the statistical test that fits it.
categories: Independent t-test
Paired t-test
Factorial ANOVA
Repeated-measures ANOVA
questions.explanation: Design dictates the test: between-subjects pairs use an independent t-test, within-subjects pairs use a paired t-test, crossed IVs use factorial ANOVA, and three within conditions use repeated-measures ANOVA.

questions.question: Match each role to its definition in an experiment.
categories: What the researcher manipulates
What the researcher measures
A level of the independent variable

correct_mapping.Condition: A level of the independent variable

questions.explanation: The IV is manipulated, the DV is measured, and a condition is one level of the IV (for example silence, background, or loud noise).

questions.question: Match each statistical concept to its definition.
categories: A false positive: detecting an effect that is not there
A false negative: missing an effect that is there
The probability of correctly detecting a real effect

correct_mapping.Type I error: A false positive: detecting an effect that is not there
correct_mapping.Type II error: A false negative: missing an effect that is there
correct_mapping.Power: The probability of correctly detecting a real effect

questions.explanation: Type I error (alpha) is a false positive, Type II error (beta) is a false negative, and power equals 1 - beta, the chance of detecting a true effect.

questions.question: Match each design type to its key property.
categories: Different participants per condition; needs about twice the sample
Same participants in all conditions; each is their own control
Groups defined by naturalistic variation, not random assignment

correct_mapping.Between-subjects: Different participants per condition; needs about twice the sample
correct_mapping.Within-subjects: Same participants in all conditions; each is their own control
correct_mapping.Quasi-experimental: Groups defined by naturalistic variation, not random assignment

questions.explanation: Between-subjects uses separate groups and a larger sample, within-subjects reuses participants for higher power, and quasi-experiments rely on pre-existing group differences.

questions.question: Match each scenario to its correct study classification.
items: Randomly assigning participants to silence vs loud noise
Correlating natural IQ with reaction time
Comparing ChatGPT users vs non-users by existing use
categories: Experimental
Correlational
Quasi-experimental
questions.explanation: Random assignment with a manipulated IV is experimental, relating two measured variables is correlational, and grouping by a pre-existing trait is quasi-experimental.

questions.question: Match each term to its description.
categories: An unrelated trial that hides the manipulation
A variable that systematically differs between conditions
Participants guessing the hypothesis and adjusting
Reversing condition order across groups

correct_mapping.Filler: An unrelated trial that hides the manipulation
correct_mapping.Confound: A variable that systematically differs between conditions
correct_mapping.Demand characteristics: Participants guessing the hypothesis and adjusting

questions.explanation: Fillers disguise the manipulation, confounds offer rival explanations, demand characteristics arise from participants inferring the aim, and counterbalancing cancels order effects.
